title: Create a Vector
scenario: You are helping a Data Analytics lecturer process student exam results for 5 students in the BUSINFO 700 paper.
task:
Create a numeric vector called scores containing the exam results: 85, 92, 78, 95, 88.
Then display the vector, confirm what type of data it is, and check how many elements it contains.

title: Descriptive Statistics
scenario: The scores vector is ready. The lecturer wants a quick statistical summary of the class results.
task:
Using the scores vector (85, 92, 78, 95, 88), calculate and display five summary statistics:
  • Average score
  • Highest score
  • Lowest score
  • Total points
  • Standard deviation

title: Character Vectors & Data Frame
scenario: You need to build a proper student record by combining names and scores into a structured table.
task:
1. Create a character vector called students with five names: Alice, Bob, Carol, David, Eve
2. Combine it with the scores vector into a structured table called student_df with columns Name and Score
3. Display the table and show its internal structure
expected:
Name Score
1   Alice    85
2     Bob    92
3   Carol    78
4   David    95
5     Eve    88
'data.frame':	5 obs. of  2 variables:
 $ Name : chr  "Alice" "Bob" "Carol" "David" ...
 $ Score: num  85 92 78 95 88

title: Logical Vectors & Subsetting
scenario: The lecturer needs to identify which students passed (score ≥ 85) and extract only their scores.
task:
From the scores vector (85, 92, 78, 95, 88):
  1. Create a logical vector that is TRUE when a score is 85 or above
  2. Display the logical vector
  3. Count how many students passed
  4. Extract and display only the passing scores
expected:
[1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE
[1] 4
[1] 85 92 95 88

title: Factor Variables
scenario: Students received letter grades. Categorical data needs special treatment in R.
task:
1. Create a character vector grades containing: A, B, B, A, A
2. Convert it to a categorical variable called grades_f
3. Confirm the data type, list the unique categories, and count how many appear in each category
expected:
[1] "factor"
[1] "A" "B"
grades_f
A B 
3 2

title: Create the Café Data Frame
scenario: You are analysing staff records for an Auckland café. Build the initial dataset from raw vectors.
task:
Create the following vectors and combine them into a data frame called cafe:
  • Name: Tane, Aroha, Liam, Mia, Connor, Priya
  • Role: Barista, Manager, Barista, Cashier, Manager, Cashier — store as a categorical variable
  • Hourly_Rate: 22, 35, 20, 21, 33, 22
  • Hours_Worked: 38, 40, -2, 25, 42, 30
Display the result.
expected:
Name    Role Hourly_Rate Hours_Worked
1   Tane Barista          22           38
2  Aroha Manager          35           40
3   Liam Barista          20           -2
4    Mia Cashier          21           25
5 Connor Manager          33           42
6  Priya Cashier          22           30

title: Inspect the Factor Variable
scenario: The cafe data frame is ready. Now inspect the categorical Role column to understand its structure.
task:
Build the cafe data frame first (see Q6), then using cafe$Role:
  1. Check what type of data it is
  2. List all unique categories
  3. Count how many staff belong to each category
expected:
[1] "factor"
[1] "Barista" "Cashier" "Manager"

Barista Cashier Manager 
      2       2       2

title: Handle the Invalid Data Entry
scenario: Liam's Hours_Worked is -2, which is clearly an entry error. It needs to be flagged before analysis.
task:
Build the cafe data frame (see Q6), then find any row where Hours_Worked is negative and replace those values with a missing value marker.

Display cafe to confirm the fix.
expected:
Name    Role Hourly_Rate Hours_Worked
1   Tane Barista          22           38
2  Aroha Manager          35           40
3   Liam Barista          20           NA
4    Mia Cashier          21           25
5 Connor Manager          33           42
6  Priya Cashier          22           30

title: Filter Out the NA Row
scenario: Liam's row is now flagged with a missing value. Remove it temporarily to clean the dataset before analysis.
task: Start with the cafe data frame after Q8 (Liam's row has NA). Remove any row where Hours_Worked is missing, then display the cleaned data frame.
expected:
Name    Role Hourly_Rate Hours_Worked
1   Tane Barista          22           38
2  Aroha Manager          35           40
3    Mia Cashier          21           25
4 Connor Manager          33           42
5  Priya Cashier          22           30

title: Add the Corrected Row Back
scenario: Liam's correct hours have been confirmed as 15. He needs to be added back to the dataset.
task:
Starting from the 5-row cafe (after Q9), append Liam's corrected record:
Name="Liam", Role="Barista", Hourly_Rate=20, Hours_Worked=15

Make sure Role is still stored as a categorical variable, then display the result.
expected:
Name    Role Hourly_Rate Hours_Worked
1   Tane Barista          22           38
2  Aroha Manager          35           40
3    Mia Cashier          21           25
4 Connor Manager          33           42
5  Priya Cashier          22           30
6   Liam Barista          20           15

title: Add Computed Columns
scenario: With the clean 6-row dataset, calculate payroll figures and categorise pay levels.
task:
Using the full 6-row cafe (Liam's Hours_Worked = 15), add three new columns in a single step:
  1. Weekly_Pay = Hourly_Rate × Hours_Worked
  2. Annual_Pay = Weekly_Pay × 52
  3. Pay_Level = "High" if Weekly_Pay ≥ $600, otherwise "Low" — store as a categorical variable
Display the result.
expected:
Name    Role Hourly_Rate Hours_Worked Weekly_Pay Annual_Pay Pay_Level
1   Tane Barista          22           38        836      43472      High
2  Aroha Manager          35           40       1400      72800      High
3   Liam Barista          20           15        300      15600       Low
4    Mia Cashier          21           25        525      27300       Low
5 Connor Manager          33           42       1386      72072      High
6  Priya Cashier          22           30        660      34320      High

title: Multi-tier Recode
scenario: HR wants to classify staff by seniority based on their hourly rate to plan training budgets.
task:
Add a new column Experience_Level based on hourly rate:
  • Under $22/hr → "Junior"
  • $22–$29/hr → "Mid"
  • $30+/hr → "Senior"
Store as a categorical variable and display the result.
expected:
Name    Role Hourly_Rate Hours_Worked Weekly_Pay Annual_Pay Pay_Level Experience_Level
1   Tane Barista          22           38        836      43472      High              Mid
2  Aroha Manager          35           40       1400      72800      High           Senior
3   Liam Barista          20           15        300      15600       Low           Junior
4    Mia Cashier          21           25        525      27300       Low           Junior
5 Connor Manager          33           42       1386      72072      High           Senior
6  Priya Cashier          22           30        660      34320      High              Mid

title: Select Specific Columns
scenario: The payroll report only needs 4 columns. Extract just those from the full dataset.
task:
From the full cafe data frame (after Q12), extract only these four columns:
Name, Weekly_Pay, Pay_Level, Experience_Level

Find two different ways to do this.
expected:
Name Weekly_Pay Pay_Level Experience_Level
1   Tane        836      High              Mid
2  Aroha       1400      High           Senior
3   Liam        300       Low           Junior
4    Mia        525       Low           Junior
5 Connor       1386      High           Senior
6  Priya        660      High              Mid

title: Sort the Data Frame
scenario: The manager wants to see who earns the most and also review staff grouped by experience tier.
task:
Sort the full cafe data frame (after Q12) two ways:

(a) By weekly earnings — highest to lowest
(b) By seniority level alphabetically, then by weekly earnings highest to lowest within each group
expected:
# (a) By Weekly_Pay descending:
    Name    Role Hourly_Rate Hours_Worked Weekly_Pay Annual_Pay Pay_Level Experience_Level
1  Aroha Manager          35           40       1400      72800      High           Senior
2 Connor Manager          33           42       1386      72072      High           Senior
3   Tane Barista          22           38        836      43472      High              Mid
4  Priya Cashier          22           30        660      34320      High              Mid
5    Mia Cashier          21           25        525      27300       Low           Junior
6   Liam Barista          20           15        300      15600       Low           Junior

# (b) By Experience_Level alphabetically, then Weekly_Pay descending:
    Name    Role Hourly_Rate Hours_Worked Weekly_Pay Annual_Pay Pay_Level Experience_Level
1    Mia Cashier          21           25        525      27300       Low           Junior
2   Liam Barista          20           15        300      15600       Low           Junior
3   Tane Barista          22           38        836      43472      High              Mid
4  Priya Cashier          22           30        660      34320      High              Mid
5  Aroha Manager          35           40       1400      72800      High           Senior
6 Connor Manager          33           42       1386      72072      High           Senior

title: Bonus: Complex Filtering
scenario: Final challenge combining everything from Lab 2. Three separate tasks to complete on the full café dataset.
task:
(a) Show only staff who have High pay AND are not Managers

(b) Calculate the average weekly pay across all 6 staff

(c) Add a Bonus column: 10% of weekly pay for Senior staff, 5% for everyone else. Display only Name, Experience_Level, Weekly_Pay, and Bonus.
expected:
# (a) High pay, not Manager:
   Name    Role Hourly_Rate Hours_Worked Weekly_Pay Annual_Pay Pay_Level Experience_Level
1  Tane Barista          22           38        836      43472      High              Mid
2 Priya Cashier          22           30        660      34320      High              Mid

# (b) Average weekly pay:
[1] 851.1667

# (c) Bonus:
    Name Experience_Level Weekly_Pay  Bonus
1   Tane              Mid        836  41.80
2  Aroha           Senior       1400 140.00
3   Liam           Junior        300  15.00
4    Mia           Junior        525  26.25
5 Connor           Senior       1386 138.60
6  Priya              Mid        660  33.00

title: Create the Sales Data Frame
scenario: You are analysing daily product sales for a small Auckland clothing store. Build the initial dataset from raw vectors.
task:
Create the following vectors and combine them into a data frame called sales:
  • Product: Jacket, Shoes, Hat, Scarf, Gloves, Jacket, Shoes
  • Category: Clothing, Footwear, Clothing, Accessories, Accessories, Clothing, Footwear — store as a categorical variable
  • Price: 120, 85, 25, 30, 20, 115, 90
  • Qty_Sold: 3, 5, 8, -1, 6, 4, 3
Display the result.
expected:
Product    Category Price Qty_Sold
1  Jacket    Clothing   120        3
2   Shoes    Footwear    85        5
3     Hat    Clothing    25        8
4   Scarf Accessories    30       -1
5  Gloves Accessories    20        6
6  Jacket    Clothing   115        4
7   Shoes    Footwear    90        3

title: Inspect the Category Factor
scenario: The sales data frame is ready. Inspect the Category column to understand its structure.
task:
Build the sales data frame (see Q17), then using sales$Category:
  1. Check what type of data it is
  2. List all unique categories
  3. Count how many products fall in each category
expected:
[1] "factor"
[1] "Accessories" "Clothing"    "Footwear"   

Accessories    Clothing    Footwear 
          2           3           2

title: Handle the Invalid Entry
scenario: Scarf's Qty_Sold is -1, which is clearly an entry error. Flag it as missing before analysis.
task:
Build the sales data frame (see Q17), then find any row where Qty_Sold is negative and replace those values with a missing value marker.

Display sales to confirm the fix.
expected:
Product    Category Price Qty_Sold
1  Jacket    Clothing   120        3
2   Shoes    Footwear    85        5
3     Hat    Clothing    25        8
4   Scarf Accessories    30       NA
5  Gloves Accessories    20        6
6  Jacket    Clothing   115        4
7   Shoes    Footwear    90        3

title: Filter NA and Add Revenue
scenario: Scarf's row is now flagged with NA. Remove it and calculate revenue for the remaining products.
task:
Start with sales after Q19 (Scarf has NA). Remove any row where Qty_Sold is missing, then add a Revenue column (Price × Qty_Sold).

Display the result.
expected:
Product    Category Price Qty_Sold Revenue
1  Jacket    Clothing   120        3     360
2   Shoes    Footwear    85        5     425
3     Hat    Clothing    25        8     200
4  Gloves Accessories    20        6     120
5  Jacket    Clothing   115        4     460
6   Shoes    Footwear    90        3     270

title: Multi-tier Price Recode
scenario: Marketing wants to classify products by price tier for promotional planning.
task:
Add a Price_Tier column based on unit price:
  • Under $30 → "Budget"
  • $30–$79 → "Mid-range"
  • $80+ → "Premium"
Store as a categorical variable and display the result.
expected:
Product    Category Price Qty_Sold Revenue Price_Tier
1  Jacket    Clothing   120        3     360    Premium
2   Shoes    Footwear    85        5     425    Premium
3     Hat    Clothing    25        8     200     Budget
4  Gloves Accessories    20        6     120     Budget
5  Jacket    Clothing   115        4     460    Premium
6   Shoes    Footwear    90        3     270    Premium

title: Sort the Sales Data
scenario: The store manager wants two sorted views of the sales data.
task:
Sort the full sales data frame (after Q21) two ways:

(a) By revenue — highest to lowest
(b) By category alphabetically, then revenue highest to lowest within each group
expected:
# (a) By Revenue descending:
  Product    Category Price Qty_Sold Revenue Price_Tier
1  Jacket    Clothing   115        4     460    Premium
2   Shoes    Footwear    85        5     425    Premium
3  Jacket    Clothing   120        3     360    Premium
4   Shoes    Footwear    90        3     270    Premium
5     Hat    Clothing    25        8     200     Budget
6  Gloves Accessories    20        6     120     Budget

# (b) By Category alphabetically, then Revenue descending:
  Product    Category Price Qty_Sold Revenue Price_Tier
1  Gloves Accessories    20        6     120     Budget
2  Jacket    Clothing   115        4     460    Premium
3  Jacket    Clothing   120        3     360    Premium
4     Hat    Clothing    25        8     200     Budget
5   Shoes    Footwear    85        5     425    Premium
6   Shoes    Footwear    90        3     270    Premium

title: Bonus: Group Summaries
scenario: Final challenge: summarise the sales data by group to support business decisions.
task:
Using the full sales data frame (after Q21):

(a) Calculate total revenue and total items sold by Category
(b) Find the average price of Premium items only
expected:
# (a) Revenue and items by Category:
     Category Total_Revenue Items_Sold
1 Accessories           120          6
2    Clothing          1020         15
3    Footwear           695          8

# (b) Average price of Premium items:
  Avg_Price
1     102.5

title: Create the Gym Data Frame
scenario: You are analysing membership data for an Auckland gym. Build the initial dataset from raw vectors.
task:
Create the following vectors and combine them into a data frame called gym:
  • Member: Sam, Jess, Mike, Sara, Luke, Amy
  • Plan: Basic, Premium, Basic, Elite, Premium, Elite — store as a categorical variable
  • Monthly_Fee: 25, 55, 25, 85, 55, 85
  • Visits: 8, 15, -3, 22, 10, 18
Display the result.
expected:
Member    Plan Monthly_Fee Visits
1    Sam   Basic          25      8
2   Jess Premium          55     15
3   Mike   Basic          25     -3
4   Sara   Elite          85     22
5   Luke Premium          55     10
6    Amy   Elite          85     18

title: Inspect the Plan Factor
scenario: The gym data frame is ready. Inspect the Plan column to understand membership distribution.
task:
Build the gym data frame (see Q24), then using gym$Plan:
  1. Check what type of data it is
  2. List all membership tiers
  3. Count how many members are on each plan
expected:
[1] "factor"
[1] "Basic"   "Elite"   "Premium"

Basic Elite Premium 
    2     2       2

title: Handle the Invalid Visit Count
scenario: Mike's Visits is -3, which is impossible. Flag it as missing before analysis.
task:
Build the gym data frame (see Q24), then find any row where Visits is negative and replace those values with a missing value marker.

Display gym to confirm the fix.
expected:
Member    Plan Monthly_Fee Visits
1    Sam   Basic          25      8
2   Jess Premium          55     15
3   Mike   Basic          25     NA
4   Sara   Elite          85     22
5   Luke Premium          55     10
6    Amy   Elite          85     18

title: Filter NA and Add Annual Revenue
scenario: Mike's row is flagged. Remove it and calculate how much each member generates annually.
task:
Start with gym after Q26 (Mike has NA). Remove any row where Visits is missing, then add an Annual_Revenue column (Monthly_Fee × 12).

Display the result.
expected:
Member    Plan Monthly_Fee Visits Annual_Revenue
1    Sam   Basic          25      8            300
2   Jess Premium          55     15            660
3   Sara   Elite          85     22           1020
4   Luke Premium          55     10            660
5    Amy   Elite          85     18           1020

title: Classify Activity Level
scenario: The gym wants to segment members by how actively they use the facilities.
task:
Add an Activity_Level column based on monthly visits:
  • Fewer than 10 visits → "Low"
  • 10–19 visits → "Moderate"
  • 20+ visits → "High"
Store as a categorical variable and display the result.
expected:
Member    Plan Monthly_Fee Visits Annual_Revenue Activity_Level
1    Sam   Basic          25      8            300            Low
2   Jess Premium          55     15            660       Moderate
3   Sara   Elite          85     22           1020           High
4   Luke Premium          55     10            660       Moderate
5    Amy   Elite          85     18           1020       Moderate

title: Bonus: Plan & Activity Analysis
scenario: Final challenge: summarise gym data to understand which plans drive the most revenue and engagement.
task:
Using the full gym data frame (after Q28):

(a) Calculate average visits and total annual revenue by Plan
(b) Show only members with Moderate or High activity (Name, Plan, Visits, Activity_Level)
expected:
# (a) By Plan:
     Plan Avg_Visits Total_Annual_Revenue
1   Basic        8.0                  300
2   Elite       20.0                 2040
3 Premium       12.5                 1320

# (b) Moderate or High activity members:
  Member    Plan Visits Activity_Level
1   Jess Premium     15       Moderate
2   Sara   Elite     22           High
3   Luke Premium     10       Moderate
4    Amy   Elite     18       Moderate

title: Create the Store Data Frame
scenario: You are analysing shelf products for a supermarket. Build the product dataset from raw vectors.
task:
Create the following vectors and combine them into a data frame called store:
  • Product: Milk, Bread, Coffee, Tea, Juice, Butter
  • Aisle: Dairy, Bakery, Beverages, Beverages, Beverages, Dairy — store as a categorical variable
  • Unit_Price: 3.50, 2.80, 12.00, 5.50, 4.20, 4.80
  • Units_Sold: 145, 98, 34, -8, 72, 56
Display the result.
expected:
Product     Aisle Unit_Price Units_Sold
1    Milk     Dairy       3.50        145
2   Bread    Bakery       2.80         98
3  Coffee Beverages      12.00         34
4     Tea Beverages       5.50         -8
5   Juice Beverages       4.20         72
6  Butter     Dairy       4.80         56

title: Inspect the Aisle Factor
scenario: The store data frame is ready. Inspect the Aisle column to understand the store layout.
task:
Build the store data frame (see Q30), then using store$Aisle:
  1. Check what type of data it is
  2. List all unique aisles
  3. Count how many products are in each aisle
expected:
[1] "factor"
[1] "Bakery"    "Beverages" "Dairy"    

   Bakery Beverages     Dairy 
        1         3         2

title: Handle the Invalid Units Sold
scenario: Tea's Units_Sold is -8, which is impossible. Flag it as missing.
task:
Build the store data frame (see Q30), then find any row where Units_Sold is negative and replace those values with a missing value marker.

Display store to confirm the fix.
expected:
Product     Aisle Unit_Price Units_Sold
1    Milk     Dairy       3.50        145
2   Bread    Bakery       2.80         98
3  Coffee Beverages      12.00         34
4     Tea Beverages       5.50         NA
5   Juice Beverages       4.20         72
6  Butter     Dairy       4.80         56

title: Filter NA and Add Total Sales
scenario: Tea's row is flagged. Remove it and calculate total sales for each remaining product.
task:
Start with store after Q32 (Tea has NA). Remove any row where Units_Sold is missing, then add a Total_Sales column (Unit_Price × Units_Sold).

Display the result.
expected:
Product     Aisle Unit_Price Units_Sold Total_Sales
1    Milk     Dairy       3.50        145       507.5
2   Bread    Bakery       2.80         98       274.4
3  Coffee Beverages      12.00         34       408.0
4   Juice Beverages       4.20         72       302.4
5  Butter     Dairy       4.80         56       268.8

scenario: The store wants to classify products for promotional pricing decisions.
task:
Add a Value_Category column based on unit price:
  • Under $5 → "Economy"
  • $5–$9.99 → "Standard"
  • $10+ → "Premium"
Store as a categorical variable and display the result.
expected:
Product     Aisle Unit_Price Units_Sold Total_Sales Value_Category
1    Milk     Dairy       3.50        145       507.5        Economy
2   Bread    Bakery       2.80         98       274.4        Economy

4   Juice Beverages       4.20         72       302.4        Economy

scenario: The store manager needs two views to optimise product placement and purchasing decisions.
task:
Using the full store data frame (after Q34):

(a) Sort by Total_Sales — highest to lowest
(b) Calculate total aisle revenue and number of products per Aisle
expected:
# (a) Sort by Total_Sales descending:
  Product     Aisle Unit_Price Units_Sold Total_Sales Value_Category

title: Create the Employee Data Frame
scenario: You are analysing an employee satisfaction survey for a mid-sized company. This dataset has 5 columns and includes a data entry error.
task:
Create the following vectors and combine them into a data frame called employees:
  • Employee_ID: E001, E002, E003, E004, E005, E006, E007
  • Department: Sales, IT, HR, Sales, IT, Finance, HR — store as a categorical variable
  • Salary: 65000, 95000, 58000, 72000, 88000, 78000, -1000
  • Satisfaction: 7, 8, 5, 9, 6, 8, 4 (scale 1–10)
  • Years_Service: 3, 7, 2, 5, 4, 6, 1
Display the result.
expected:
Employee_ID Department Salary Satisfaction Years_Service
1        E001      Sales  65000            7             3
2        E002         IT  95000            8             7
3        E003         HR  58000            5             2
4        E004      Sales  72000            9             5
5        E005         IT  88000            6             4
6        E006    Finance  78000            8             6
7        E007         HR  -1000            4             1

title: Inspect the Data Frame Structure
scenario: Before cleaning, get a full picture of the dataset: its size, column names, and department distribution.
task:
Build the employees data frame (see Q36), then:
  1. How many rows does it have?
  2. How many columns?
  3. What are the column names?
  4. What are the unique Department levels?
  5. How many employees are in each department?
expected:
[1] 7
[1] 5
[1] "Employee_ID"   "Department"    "Salary"        "Satisfaction"  "Years_Service"
[1] "Finance" "HR"      "IT"      "Sales"  
Finance      HR      IT   Sales 
      1       2       2       2

title: Remove the Invalid Salary
scenario: E007's salary of -1000 is clearly an entry error. Flag it and remove the row before analysis.
task:
Build the employees data frame (see Q36).
Set any negative Salary to NA, then remove rows with missing salary using base R subsetting (employees[!is.na(...), ]).

Display the cleaned data frame.
expected:
Employee_ID Department Salary Satisfaction Years_Service
1        E001      Sales  65000            7             3
2        E002         IT  95000            8             7
3        E003         HR  58000            5             2
4        E004      Sales  72000            9             5
5        E005         IT  88000            6             4
6        E006    Finance  78000            8             6

title: Add Tax, Net Salary, and Seniority
scenario: HR needs payroll figures and a seniority classification for all 6 employees.
task:
Starting from the cleaned 6-row employees (after Q38), add three columns in a single mutate():
  1. Tax = Salary × 20%
  2. Net_Salary = Salary × 80%
  3. Seniority = "Junior" if Years_Service < 3, "Mid" if 3–5, "Senior" if 6+ — store as a categorical variable
Display the result.
expected:
Employee_ID Department Salary Satisfaction Years_Service   Tax Net_Salary Seniority
1        E001      Sales  65000            7             3 13000      52000       Mid
2        E002         IT  95000            8             7 19000      76000    Senior
3        E003         HR  58000            5             2 11600      46400    Junior
4        E004      Sales  72000            9             5 14400      57600       Mid
5        E005         IT  88000            6             4 17600      70400       Mid
6        E006    Finance  78000            8             6 15600      62400    Senior

title: Select and Sort
scenario: The payroll report needs a clean 5-column view sorted by department and salary.
task:
From the full employees data frame (after Q39), select only: Employee_ID, Department, Salary, Net_Salary, Seniority.

Then sort by Department alphabetically, then Salary highest to lowest within each department.
expected:
Employee_ID Department Salary Net_Salary Seniority
1        E006    Finance  78000      62400    Senior
2        E003         HR  58000      46400    Junior
3        E002         IT  95000      76000    Senior
4        E005         IT  88000      70400       Mid
5        E004      Sales  72000      57600       Mid
6        E001      Sales  65000      52000       Mid

title: Department Summary
scenario: Leadership wants a department-level summary combining headcount, pay, and satisfaction.
task:
Using the full employees data frame (after Q39), group by Department and calculate:
  • Count — number of employees
  • Avg_Salary — mean salary
  • Avg_Satisfaction — mean satisfaction score
expected:
Department Count Avg_Salary Avg_Satisfaction
1    Finance     1      78000                8
2         HR     1      58000                5
3         IT     2      91500                7
4      Sales     2      68500                8

title: Bonus: Performance Scoring & Top Earners
scenario: Final challenge combining filtering, computed columns, and ranking on the employee dataset.
task:
Using the full employees data frame (after Q39):

(a) Add a Performance_Score column (Satisfaction × Years_Service). Filter to show only IT or Finance employees earning more than $80,000 — display Employee_ID, Department, Salary, Satisfaction, Performance_Score.

(b) Find the single top performer overall (highest Performance_Score) and display their row (Employee_ID, Department, Satisfaction, Years_Service, Performance_Score).
expected:
# (a) IT or Finance with Salary > $80,000:
  Employee_ID Department Salary Satisfaction Performance_Score
1        E002         IT  95000            8                56
2        E005         IT  88000            6                24

# (b) Top performer overall:
  Employee_ID Department Satisfaction Years_Service Performance_Score
1        E002         IT            8             7                56

title: Monte Carlo — Basic Setup
scenario: You are building a Monte Carlo simulation in Excel to model Emily's superannuation growth under uncertainty. Emily earns $60,000/year with a standard deviation of $8,000.
task:
Setup steps:
  1. In cell B1: mean salary = 60000
  2. In cell B2: std dev = 8000
  3. In A2:A1001, enter a formula to generate 1,000 random draws from this normal distribution
  4. Press F9 to recalculate and observe the distribution changing
expected:
# In Excel — A2:A1001 should produce values near 60,000
# Mean of simulation ≈ 60,000
# Std Dev of simulation ≈ 8,000
# Press F9 repeatedly to see values change each time

title: Scenario Analysis
scenario: Emily's growth rate varies by economic scenario. Model three scenarios with Excel formulas.
task:
In Excel:
  1. Cell B5: type a scenario label (base, pessimistic, or optimistic)
  2. Base: salary grows by 2%
  3. Pessimistic: salary falls by 2%
  4. Optimistic: salary grows by 5%
  5. Write a conditional formula in C5 that returns the correct salary based on B5
expected:
# When B5 = "base"       → C5 shows salary × 1.02
# When B5 = "pessimistic" → C5 shows salary × 0.98
# When B5 = "optimistic"  → C5 shows salary × 1.05

title: Glide Path — Asset Allocation
scenario: Emily is 30 years old. As she ages, her portfolio should shift from high-growth (SkyHigh) to stable (HarbourSafe) funds — a strategy called a Glide Path.
task:
Build a table with rows for age 30 to 65:
  1. Column A: Age
  2. Column B: SkyHigh allocation — starts at 100%, decreases linearly to 0% by age 65
  3. Column C: HarbourSafe allocation — the remainder
  4. Column D: Expected annual return — SkyHigh earns 9%, HarbourSafe earns 4%
  5. Column E: Portfolio value — starts at $50,000, grows by return + $5,000/year contribution

scenario: Week 3 Excel content will cover advanced Monte Carlo scenarios including retirement risk modelling.
task: Check back after Week 3 lectures for the full simulation assignment prep.
